evidence_points: Diagnose CKD when reduced eGFR and/or markers of kidney damage persist for at least 3 months.
Stage CKD using both G-stage (eGFR) and A-stage (ACR).
Use CKD-EPI equation for eGFR reporting in adults.
Use urine ACR for proteinuria assessment; ACR >=3 mg/mmol is clinically significant and ACR >=70 mg/mmol is severe proteinuria.
Assess progression with eGFR trend and ACR trend, not single values alone.
Use KFRE in CKD stages G3a-G5 to support referral decisions; 5-year kidney failure risk >5% supports renal referral.
Refer urgently for suspected rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis/vasculitis, severe hyperkalaemia, or acute-on-chronic deterioration requiring same-day specialist input.
Treat cardiovascular and renal risk aggressively (BP control, RAAS blockade where indicated, SGLT2 inhibitor use when eligible, statin therapy).
summary: Referral combines risk tools (KFRE), severity, and progression
Primary care treatment is risk-factor and renoprotective management
